data.title: Is EPAM Systems (EPAM) Share Price Misaligned With Its Cash Flow Outlook?
data.description:
• If you are looking at EPAM Systems and wondering whether the current share price reflects its true worth, the valuation picture is more nuanced than a quick glance at the chart suggests.
  • The stock last closed at US$137.02, with a 0.6% move over the past week, a 6.1% decline over 30 days, and returns of 31.6% decline year to date and 5.5% decline over the past year, following even steeper 3-year and 5-year declines of 52.9% and 67.7% respectively.
  • These moves have kept EPAM on the radar for investors watching how the IT services sector adjusts to changing enterprise spending and shifting project priorities. While there has not been a single headline event driving these figures, the stock continues to react to ongoing sentiment around demand for software development, digital transformation work, and how companies allocate technology budgets.
  • Simply Wall St currently gives EPAM a valuation score of 4 out of 6, which reflects checks where the shares appear undervalued and others where they do not. The sections that follow walk through the standard valuation approaches before finishing with a broader framework that can help you judge whether the market price makes sense for you.

Find out why EPAM Systems's -5.5% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Approach 1: EPAM Systems Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting the cash it may generate in the future and then discounting those cash flows back to today.

For EPAM Systems, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow stands at about $617.2 million. Analyst inputs and subsequent extrapolations point to projected Free Cash Flow of $699.0 million by 2028, with further projections out to 2035 based on gradually changing growth assumptions supplied by Simply Wall St.

Using these cash flow projections and discounting them, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$221.61 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$137.02, this implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 38.2%, which indicates that the shares screen as undervalued under this specific DCF framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for EPAM Systems.

Approach 2: EPAM Systems Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like EPAM Systems, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. In general, higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tend to justify a lower multiple.

EPAM currently trades on a P/E of 19.6x. That sits above the peer group average of 13.1x, and slightly below the broader IT industry average of 20.1x. On those simple comparisons alone, the shares look more expensive than peers but roughly aligned with the sector.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for EPAM is 27.1x. This is a proprietary estimate of what the P/E might be given factors such as the company’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market value and specific risks. Because it is tailored to EPAM’s characteristics rather than broad group averages, it can offer a more company specific benchmark than a simple peer or industry comparison. With the Fair Ratio above the current 19.6x P/E, this framework suggests the shares screen as undervalued on an earnings multiple basis.

The platform updates your view automatically when new news or earnings arrive. data.title: With EPS Growth And More, Agro Phos (India) (NSE:AGROPHOS) Makes An Interesting Case
data.description:
Investors are often guided by the idea of discovering 'the next big thing', even if that means buying 'story stocks' without any revenue, let alone profit. But as Peter Lynch said in One Up On Wall Street, 'Long shots almost never pay off.' A loss-making company is yet to prove itself with profit, and eventually the inflow of external capital may dry up.

So if this idea of high risk and high reward doesn't suit, you might be more interested in profitable, growing companies, like Agro Phos (India) (NSE:AGROPHOS). Even if this company is fairly valued by the market, investors would agree that generating consistent profits will continue to provide Agro Phos (India) with the means to add long-term value to shareholders.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

How Quickly Is Agro Phos (India) Increasing Earnings Per Share?

If you believe that markets are even vaguely efficient, then over the long term you'd expect a company's share price to follow its earnings per share (EPS) outcomes. So it makes sense that experienced investors pay close attention to company EPS when undertaking investment research. Impressively, Agro Phos (India) has grown EPS by 19% per year, compound, in the last three years. If growth like this continues on into the future, then shareholders will have plenty to smile about.

Top-line growth is a great indicator that growth is sustainable, and combined with a high earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margin, it's a great way for a company to maintain a competitive advantage in the market. Agro Phos (India) shareholders can take confidence from the fact that EBIT margins are up from 5.4% to 13%, and revenue is growing. That's great to see, on both counts.

You can take a look at the company's revenue and earnings growth trend, in the chart below. Click on the chart to see the exact numbers.

See our latest analysis for Agro Phos (India)

Agro Phos (India) isn't a huge company, given its market capitalisation of ₹626m. That makes it extra important to check on its balance sheet strength.

Are Agro Phos (India) Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

Many consider high insider ownership to be a strong sign of alignment between the leaders of a company and the ordinary shareholders. So we're pleased to report that Agro Phos (India) insiders own a meaningful share of the business. Actually, with 44% of the company to their names, insiders are profoundly invested in the business. Those who are comforted by solid insider ownership like this should be happy, as it implies that those running the business are genuinely motivated to create shareholder value. Valued at only ₹626m Agro Phos (India) is really small for a listed company. So despite a large proportional holding, insiders only have ₹278m worth of stock. That's not a huge stake in absolute terms, but it should help keep insiders aligned with other shareholders.

Does Agro Phos (India) Deserve A Spot On Your Watchlist?

For growth investors, Agro Phos (India)'s raw rate of earnings growth is a beacon in the night. With EPS growth rates like that, it's hardly surprising to see company higher-ups place confidence in the company through continuing to hold a significant investment. On the balance of its merits, solid EPS growth and company insiders who are aligned with the shareholders would indicate a business that is worthy of further research. Before you take the next step you should know about the 3 warning signs for Agro Phos (India) (2 shouldn't be ignored!) that we have uncovered.

There's always the possibility of doing well buying stocks that are not growing earnings and do not have insiders buying shares. But for those who consider these important metrics, we encourage you to check out companies that do have those features. You can access a tailored list of Indian companies which have demonstrated growth backed by significant insider holdings.

data.description:
Investors are often guided by the idea of discovering 'the next big thing', even if that means buying 'story stocks' without any revenue, let alone profit. Unfortunately, these high risk investments often have little probability of ever paying off, and many investors pay a price to learn their lesson. Loss-making companies are always racing against time to reach financial sustainability, so investors in these companies may be taking on more risk than they should.

If this kind of company isn't your style, you like companies that generate revenue, and even earn profits, then you may well be interested in JMDC (TSE:4483). Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

JMDC's Earnings Per Share Are Growing

If you believe that markets are even vaguely efficient, then over the long term you'd expect a company's share price to follow its earnings per share (EPS) outcomes. Therefore, there are plenty of investors who like to buy shares in companies that are growing EPS. Shareholders will be happy to know that JMDC's EPS has grown 19% each year, compound, over three years. As a general rule, we'd say that if a company can keep up that sort of growth, shareholders will be beaming.

One way to double-check a company's growth is to look at how its revenue, and earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins are changing. The good news is that JMDC is growing revenues, and EBIT margins improved by 4.7 percentage points to 22%, over the last year. Ticking those two boxes is a good sign of growth, in our book.

It should give investors a sense of security owning shares in a company if insiders also own shares, creating a close alignment their interests. JMDC followers will find comfort in knowing that insiders have a significant amount of capital that aligns their best interests with the wider shareholder group. Indeed, they hold JP¥4.0b worth of its stock. This considerable investment should help drive long-term value in the business. Even though that's only about 1.7% of the company, it's enough money to indicate alignment between the leaders of the business and ordinary shareholders.

Is JMDC Worth Keeping An Eye On?

If you believe that share price follows earnings per share you should definitely be delving further into JMDC's strong EPS growth. With EPS growth rates like that, it's hardly surprising to see company higher-ups place confidence in the company through continuing to hold a significant investment. Migdal Insurance and Financial Holdings (TASE:MGDL) recently reported full year 2025 earnings, with net income of ₪1,832 million and basic earnings per share from continuing operations of ₪1.73.

Those figures compare with net income of ₪1,419 million and basic earnings per share of ₪1.35 a year earlier, giving investors fresh numbers to weigh against the stock’s recent price moves.

The latest earnings arrive in a mixed price backdrop, with a 1 day share price return of 2.24% and a 30 day share price decline of 14.52%. However, a very large 1 year total shareholder return suggests longer term momentum remains strong.

If Migdal’s moves have you thinking about where else capital might work hard for you, this could be a good moment to scan for financial groups within 96 top founder-led companies

With earnings per share at ₪1.73 and a 1 year total shareholder return that is very large, alongside a recent 14.52% 30 day decline, is Migdal now trading below its worth or has the market already priced in future growth?

Preferred P/E of 9.5x: Is it justified?

Migdal is trading on a P/E of 9.5x, which sits below both the Israeli market and local insurance peers, even after a very large 1 year total return.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share, so it gives you a quick sense of how much investors are paying for each ₪1 of profit.

For Migdal, a 9.5x P/E alongside earnings growth of 5.4% per year over 5 years and improved net profit margins from 12.7% to 14.9% suggests the market is not paying a premium for that track record. The SWS DCF model, however, estimates a future cash flow value of ₪8.69. At a last close of ₪16.42 the share price sits well above that cash flow based fair value and points to a very different signal from the earnings multiple.

Compared with the Israeli market P/E of 15.7x, Migdal sits at a clear discount. It also trades below the Asian insurance industry average of 11.2x and a peer average of 11.1x. Those gaps indicate investors are paying less for Migdal’s earnings than for both the wider market and closer peers, even though earnings growth over the past year of 29.1% exceeded its own 5 year average.
